narration: Your bank hit you with an overdraft fee — but here's the thing, it probably wasn't your fault. Banks secretly reorder your transactions to drain $12 billion a year from customers.
visual: Bank building SVG with dollar signs raining down into a vault. Counter ticks up: '$12,000,000,000 COLLECTED IN OVERDRAFT FEES'. Red accent badge pulses.

narration: Here's how the trick works. Say you had $200 in your account and made five purchases — a $180 rent payment, then four small ones. Normal, right?
visual: Checking account balance shows $200. Five transaction cards slide in sequentially: $180 rent, $12 coffee, $8 lunch, $6 snack, $5 app. All look fine.

narration: Your bank secretly flips those transactions — largest first. The $180 hits, wipes your account, and suddenly every small purchase is an overdraft. One day, five fees.
visual: Transactions reorder before your eyes: $180 jumps to top, balance drops to $20, then red X marks explode on each smaller transaction. '$35 FEE' badges stack up.

narration: Instead of one overdraft, you get five. That's $175 in fees on a day you were only $25 short. Banks call it 'high-to-low processing' — wild, right?
visual: Calculator SVG shows the math: 5 × $35 = $175 in red. Counter animates from $0 to $175. Side note: 'YOU WERE ONLY $25 SHORT' in amber.

narration: Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and JPMorgan Chase have all been sued over this — and paid out over $1.4 billion in settlements. They kept doing it anyway.
visual: Three bank logos (represented as shield icons) each get a gavel strike. Settlement amount '$1.4 BILLION' appears below. 'STILL DOING IT' badge in red.

narration: Turn on low-balance alerts. Link a savings account as overdraft backup. Or switch to a credit union — they charge $0 to $10. Don't let them engineer your next fee.
visual: Phone with notification bell showing '$35 ALERT SAVED'. Piggy bank icon links to a shield icon with '$0 FEE'. CTA: 'FOLLOW FOR MORE MONEY TRAPS'.
